coredata.dc:title: A facile carbon dots based fluorescent probe for ultrasensitive detection of ascorbic acid in biological fluids via non-oxidation reduction strategy
coredata.dc:creator: Kong, Weiheng
Wu, Di
Li, Guoliang
Chen, Xuefeng
Gong, Peiwei
Sun, Zhiwei
Chen, Guang
Xia, Lian
You, Jinmao
Wu, Yongning
coredata.dc:description: A rapid, facile and ultrasensitive fluorescence sensing system based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-doped CDs) for the detection of ascorbic acid (AA) was developed. The highly photoluminescent N-doped CDs with excellent solubility in water and good biocompatibility were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal synthesis and gave the fluorescence quantum yield of 47%. The addition of AA can intensively suppress the fluorescence of the N-doped CDs through the synergistic effect of the inner filter effect (IFE) and the static quenching effect (SQE). Benefited from the remarkable synergistic effect of IFE and SQE, a facile and ultrasensitive sensor was constructed successfully for AA sensing. The detection procedure was achieved within 2min. The linear response range of AA was obtained from 10−3 to 10−8 M with a detection limit of 5nM. This developed method enjoyed many merits including more simplicity, lost cost, high sensitivity, good selectivity, rapid response and excellent biocompatibility. Notably, the proposed fluorescent sensor exhibits excellent performance and applicability for AA determination in human serum and rat brain microdialysate, and may provide a fast yet facile route for AA detection in physiological and pathological fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Inner filter effect
Ascorbic acid
Fluorimetric detection

Detection of AA using N-doped CD probe
Detection of AA in human serum samples and rat brain microdialysates
Characterization and properties of the CDs
Principle of AA assay based on IFE and SQE
Detection of AA with N-doped CDs
Selectivity of CDs with different ions and biological molecules
Application of the proposed sensing system to serum sample analysis
Application of the proposed sensing system to rat brain microdialysates analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of as-prepared N-doped CDs. (B) Corresponding diameter distribution histogram of the N-doped CDs. (C) XRD pattern of N-doped CDs and (D) FT-IR spectrum of N-doped CDs.
(A) XPS survey spectrum of the N-doped carbon quantum dots, (B) high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s and (C) high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s.
UV spectra (a, b) and fluorescence spectra (c, d) of N-doped CDs solution in the absence (a, c) and presence (b, d) of 100μL of ascorbic acid (10−2 M).
(A) Optimization of ascorbic acid detection assay including reaction times (from 0 to 20min) and (B) pH of solutions (pH =6.0, 6.8, 7.0 and 7.4).

(A) Fluorescence responses of N-doped CDs upon addition of various concentrations of ascorbic acid (from top to bottom 0, 10−8, 5×10−8, 10−7, 5×10−7, 10−6, 5×10−6, 10−5, 10−4 and 10−3 M) and (B) plots of F0–F versus concentration from 10−8 to 10−3 M. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of CDs in the absence and presence of ascorbic acid, respectively.
The levels of ascorbic acid in serum samples of healthy volunteers (samples 1−6) and cancer patients (samples 7−12).
The ascorbic acid level in the microdialysates from cortex of a rat brain under different physiological conditions (calm: 0–60min; surgeries: 60–80min; ischemia: 80–140min).
A schematic illustration of the synthesis of N-doped CDs and the strategy of facile ascorbic acid sensing.
Comparison of present method with some of the previously published methods.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of the concentrations of AA in human serum (healthy volunteers: samples 1, 2, 3 and cancer patients: samples 5 and 6).

head.ce:title: A facile carbon dots based fluorescent probe for ultrasensitive detection of ascorbic acid in biological fluids via non-oxidation reduction strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Sciences, Xiamen University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis of Shandong Province, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Weiheng Kong and Di Wu equally contribute to this study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A rapid, facile and ultrasensitive fluorescence sensing system based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-doped CDs) for the detection of ascorbic acid (AA) was developed. The highly photoluminescent N-doped CDs with excellent solubility in water and good biocompatibility were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal synthesis and gave the fluorescence quantum yield of 47%. The addition of AA can intensively suppress the fluorescence of the N-doped CDs through the synergistic effect of the inner filter effect (IFE) and the static quenching effect (SQE). Benefited from the remarkable synergistic effect of IFE and SQE, a facile and ultrasensitive sensor was constructed successfully for AA sensing. The detection procedure was achieved within 2min. The linear response range of AA was obtained from 10−3 to 10−8 M with a detection limit of 5nM. This developed method enjoyed many merits including more simplicity, lost cost, high sensitivity, good selectivity, rapid response and excellent biocompatibility. Notably, the proposed fluorescent sensor exhibits excellent performance and applicability for AA determination in human serum and rat brain microdialysate, and may provide a fast yet facile route for AA detection in physiological and pathological fields.

ce:section.ce:para: As a natural organic compound, ascorbic acid (AA) is an essential nutrient for humans, which can prevent free radical induced diseases, especially Parkinson's disease and cancers. Thus, a facile, rapid and simple method for AA detection is imperative for biomedical chemistry, diagnostics and pathological research [1]. Up to date, various types of approaches have been developed for AA detection. Typical strategies include chemiluminescence [2], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [3], capillary zone electrophoresis [4], enzymatic fluorimetric and electrochemical method [5,6]. Most of these methods were intricate and time-consuming, and some of these usually required specialized and expensive instruments. Compared to other detection methods, fluorescent methods have attracted more attention due to their good reproducibility and high sensitivity [7]. Recently, many novel fluorescent methods have been developed for AA sensing. For instance, Lin et.al proposed a new electrochemical method with high selectivity and reproducibility for AA sensing in vivo [8]. Li's group reported the Fe3+-functionalized CDs for detection of AA in rat brain microdialysates based on the specific redox reaction between Fe3+ ions and AA [9]. Mao's group successfully developed the mechanism of single-layer MnO2 nanosheets suppressing the fluorescence of 7-hydroxycoumarin and designed a new sensing platform for AA detection from rat brain [10]. Tang's group reported an efficient nanoprobe based on the redox reaction of AA and cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) for detection and imaging of AA in living cells and in vivo [11]. Li's group designed a novel nanoprobe for detection of AA in brain microdialysate based on CDs and modified hexagonal CoOOH nanoflakes [12]. Although these methods exhibited attractive sensitivity and selectivity, and gave the different insights for AA sensing, some technique challenges were still remained. First, most of the above nano-sensors were based on the redox reaction between fluorescent nanoprobes and AA to turn on or turn off the fluorescence. Thus, some reductive biological molecules in vivo like cysteine (Cys) and glutathione (GSH) can disturb the analytical results of AA. In addition, some of these experiments required complex preparation process such as the probe modification or the nano-architecture construction [13]. Furthermore, poor biocompatibility and solubility of some nano-sensors also limited their applications in clinic detection. Thus, a novel method for AA sensing with good biocompatibility, ultrasensitivity and specificity is still highly desired.

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) as a promising carbon material, appear to be visible substitutes for conventional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) in many fields due to their advantages including low toxicity, easy fabrication, stable photoluminescence, excellent water dispersibility and good biocompatibility [14–16]. CDs have been widely used in different fields, such as biosensors, bioimaging, photocatalysis, etc [17–19]. Nitrogen-doped CDs are more superior to traditional quantum dots with the merits of high fluorescent activity, high fluorescence quantum yield, good aqueous solubility and easy functionality, which make them more suitable for the sensitive detection [20–23].

Recently, an alternative principle for fluorescent assay design was developed using the inner filter effect (IFE) [24–26]. Generally, the IFE refers to the absorption of the excitation and/or emission of light by absorbers in the detection system, which is usually considered as an annoying source of error and should be avoided in spectrofluorometry [27–29]. IFE has grown to be an effective tool for the construction of simple, sensitive and rapid fluorescence sensors by converting the analytical absorption signals into fluorescence signals [7,30,31]. Notably, the IFE-based nanosensor does not require any covalent linking between the receptor and the fluorophore or surface modifications of fluorescence nanomaterial, which offers the considerable flexibility and more simplicity in probe design [31,32].

In this study, highly photoluminescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-doped CDs) were successfully constructed to detect AA based on synergistic effect of IFE and SQE principle ( Scheme 1). The N-doped CDs were synthesized using sodium citrate as the carbon source and tripolycyanamide as both of nitrogen source and functional monomer by one-step hydrothermal method, and gave the fluorescence quantum yield of 47%, which was helpful for acting as a highly sensitive nanosensor (Scheme 1A). The new N-doped CDs possessed high fluorescence yields and excellent aqueous solubility. Most importantly, the fluorescence of N-doped CDs can be efficiently quenched by the addition of AA. This phenomenon may be due to that hydrogen bonds were formed between the hydroxyl groups of AA and the surface functional groups of the N-doped CDs, which further induced the intensive fluorescence suppression due to the synergistic effect IFE and the SQE (Scheme 1B). Most of the previously reported works were based on redox reaction mechanism, which cannot effectively avoid the interferences from the reducing substances in real samples. Meanwhile, complex probe modification and sophisticated sample pretreatment, poor biocompatibility and low water solubility also limited their further applications and developments. However, our non-redox fluorescence strategy could effectively avoid the interference of the reducing substances and did not require the complicated pretreatment process or expensive instrumentation. After systematic optimization of the sensing conditions, the proposed strategy allowed the sensitive and selective AA detection with the exciting detection limit of 5nM (S/N =3), which was lower than most reported methods. The developed method was successfully applied for the detection of AA in biological fluid samples and rat brain microdialysates samples, and satisfactory results were achieved, indicating a great application promising in bioanalytical field.

Sodium citrate, tripolycyanamide, ascorbic acid and other salts were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Triple-distilled water was purified with a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore, Molsheim, France). Amino acids were purchased from Sigma. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) buffer solution was prepared by mixing stock solutions of Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4. All reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used as received. And all aqueous solutions were prepared with triple-distilled water.

The fluorescence spectra were obtained using a HITACHI F-7000 PC spectrofluorophotometer equipped with a Xenon lamp using right-angle geometry. Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR) spectrum was recorded with a Beifen-Ruili WQF-520A FT-IR spectrometer equipped with a DGTS detector (16 scans). The morphologies and sizes of N-doped CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi-600, Hitachi, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were performed by a PHI 5702 spectrometer. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum was recorded with RIGAKU MiniFlex600 X-Ray Diffractometer. Microdialysis experiments in vivo were by a microinjection pump (CMA/100; CMA Microdialysis AB, Stockholm, Sweden) with 2mm long microdialysis probe (Bioanalytical Systems Inc. (BAS), BAS Carnegie Medicine).

In brief, a total of 0.24g of tripolycyanamide and 0.58g of sodium citrate were dissolved into 25mL distilled water. Then the mixture was sonicated for 5min and transferred to a 50mL Teflon-lined hydrothermal reactor. After 180°C of hydrothermal reaction for 12h, the reaction solution was filtered with a cylinder membrane filter (0.22µm) to remove the large dots, and dialyzed against ultrapure water through a 500 D of dialysis membrane for 48h. The as-prepared CDs were diluted 104 times by water and stored at 4°C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of quantum yield of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Determination of the quantum yield of CDs was accomplished by comparison of the photoluminescence of CDs and absorbance values of quinine sulfate. Excitation wavelength was fixed at 356nm. Quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1M of H2SO4 and the CDs were dissolved in water. Using quinine sulfate as the standard, its quantum yield was 0.577 and refractive index was 1.33.
The quantum yield was calculated by the following equation:

φ =
φ
s
[
(
I
A
S
n
2
)
/ (
I
S
A
n
S
2
)
]
where “φ” is the quantum yield, “I” is the integrated intensity, “A” is the optical density and “n” is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript ‘‘s’’ refers to standard with the known quantum yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA using N-doped CD probe
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the sensitivity toward AA of the fluorescence probe, 100μL of various concentrations of AA (0, 10−8, 5×10−8, 10−7, 5×10−7, 10−6, 5×10−6, 10−5, 10−4 and 10−3 M) were injected into 1.0mL of N-doped CDs. Then the mixed solutions were measured using a 1cm path length quartz cell at room temperature under the fluorescence excitation wavelength at 345nm. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in human serum samples and rat brain microdialysates
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum samples were obtained from Qufu Herbalist Hospital. Blood samples were collected in an open system with no soluble anticoagulant. The blood serums was collected using 16,000rpm of centrifugation for 10min 20μL of the human serums was used for AA analysis. All studies were approved by Qufu Herbalist Hospital Medical Ethics Committee and Qufu Normal University Ethics Committee.

Male Sprague-Dawley rats (300g; specifically pathogen free) were purchased from Shandong Lukang Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. They were housed in groups on a 12h light-12h dark cycle at 25°C for one week acclimation. Animal studies were approved by a Qufu Normal University animal care committee. Each rat was euthanatized when the experiment was completed.

Briefly, the rats with guide cannula were anesthetized with chloral hydrate (350mg/kg i.p.). The microdialysis probe (2mm in length) was implanted in the striatum (the brain cortex region) at the site of 2.5mm anterior to bregma, 2.5mm lateral from midline, and 7.0mm below dura, which was perfused with artificial cerebrospinal fluid (aCSF) (126mM of NaCl, 27.5 of mM NaHCO3, 2.4mM of KCl, 1.1mM of CaCl2, 0.85mM of MgCl2, 0.5mM of Na2SO4, 0.5mM of KH2PO4, pH =7.0) at a flow rate of 3μL/min driven by a microinjection pump. At least 90min for equilibration, every 50μL of brain dialysate was obtained and used without further treatments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and properties of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology, size and microstructure of the N-doped CDs were characterized by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As displayed in Fig. 1A, the prepared N-doped CDs were well monodispersed and homogeneous, and particles dispersed in a spherical shape. The inset in Fig. 1A shows the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of N-doped CDs, the spacing of the adjacent lattice planes for random nanoparticle were 0.25nm, which agrees well with the (100) diffraction plane of graphite. This result indicated that the as-prepared CDs possess a graphite-like structure. The sizes of N-doped CDs were distributed mainly in a narrow range from 3.0 to 5.5nm, and almost 41% of the N-doped CDs were concentrated at an average size of 4.5nm by statistical analysis of more than three hundred particles (Fig. 1B).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed to get the information about the structure. The XRD pattern measured for the N-doped CDs showed a broad diffraction peak centered at (2θ) =27°, which attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms, and no extra peaks were detected (Fig. 1C). This result was consistent with the previously reported CDs [15–17]. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was employed to analyze the surface composition of the N-doped CDs. As shown in Fig. 1D, the wide peak at 3130cm−1 was corresponding to the surface-adsorbed water, amine and hydroxyl groups. These surface groups stabilized the CDs in aqueous solution. The peak at 1580cm−1 was associated with the stretching vibrations of C=O and C=C, the band at 1420cm−1 belonged to the C–N bending vibrations. The weak peak at 1024cm−1 could be attributed to C–O–C stretching vibrations.

The surface composition and elemental analysis of the N-doped CDs were carried out by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The full range XPS analysis ( Fig. 2A) showed the N-doped CDs were composed of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, which appeared at 284.8, 399.7, and 531.7eV, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2B, the high resolution C1s spectrum showed four peaks at 284.8, 285.6, 287.9, and 288.5eV, which corresponded to C–C(sp3), C–N (sp3), C=O (sp2) and C–O (sp3), respectively. The high resolution N1s spectrum further confirmed that C–N–C (398.7eV) and C–C (399.5eV) bonds exhibited in N-doped CDs (Fig. 2C), which further verified the as-synthesized CDs were nitrogen-doped CDs.

To further verify the aptitude of the prepared CDs, the good biocompatibility was evaluated on murine macrophage cells. The cytotoxicity of N-doped CDs was negligible, as confirmed by the standard methylthiazol tetrazolium (MTT) assay (Fig. S1), MTT showed the viability of cells with N-doped CDs in the range of the cytotoxicity of 5.0−100μg/mL remained unchangeable, confirming the excellent biocompatibility. The as-prepared N-doped CDs also presented excellent stability, which was examined by long-time monitoring of fluorescent intensity. Under light illumination for 1.5h, fluorescence intensity remained almost unchanged (Fig. S2), and the fluorescence intensity remained constantly even after 30 days storage (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of AA assay based on IFE and SQE
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1B, the proposed sensing strategy based on IFE and SQE was established, where N-doped CDs functioned as the fluorometric reporter, and AA served as the fluorescence quencher. Compared to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer-based methods (FRET), IFE did not require covalent chemical linkage between absorber and fluorophore [13]. Thus, the synthesized process of the fluorescent materials was simplified. However, the IFE would occur effectively only when the absorption band of the absorber possessed a complementary overlap with the excitation and/or emission bands of the fluorophore to some extent. Thus, there are some restrictions in the development of IFE-based fluorescence assays, such as the limited selection of suitable absorbers and fluorophores, small extinction coefficients of conventional absorbers, etc.

As mentioned above, it was important to choose a suitable absorber and fluorophore pair for the IFE-based fluorescent chemosensor. Interestingly, in the present study, after addition of AA, the complex of AA-CDs can be formed at the surface of CDs due to the hydrogen bonding between the hydrophilic groups of CDs and hydroxyl groups of AA. Meanwhile, a new absorption peak generated at 345nm had a good overlap with excitation spectrum of fluorescent N-doped CDs, which significantly affected the UV absorption of CDs and further inhibited the fluorescence of CDs due to IFE. To verify the possible existence of IFE between N-doped CDs and AA, we mixed N-doped CDs with 100μL of 10−2 M AA and monitored the absorbance changes of N-doped CDs. As shown in Fig. 3, the black curve (a) was the UV spectrum of N-doped CDs without of AA. A new UV spectrum peak appeared at 340nm when the AA was added into the N-doped CDs solution (Fig. 3, curve b), which had a good overlap with the excitation wavelength of N-doped CDs (345nm). Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra of CDs mixed with 10−2 M of AA were recorded. Under 345nm excitation, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 440nm was quenched significantly and the quenching efficiency could reach about 91% upon an addition of 10−2 M of AA, suggesting the powerful quenching ability of AA (Fig. 3, curve c and d). Finally, the absorbance enhancement of AA complex on the surface of CDs could be successfully converted to fluorescence quenching of CDs. Furthermore, the formation of hydrogen bonding between AA and CDs extended the spatial effect, which may also resulted in the intensive fluorescence suppression due to the static quenching effect (SQE). With the aid of the synergistic effect of IFE and SQE mechanism, a facile, rapid and sensitive approach for AA monitoring was developed. This novel sensing strategy for AA detection enjoyed many advantages: (i) the AA sensing probe can be easily prepared by a one-step hydrothermal synthesis and the raw materials were cheap, easy to get; environmental friendliness and nontoxicity; (ii) this non-redox strategy does not require complex treatment of the samples, which makes the method more simple and facile; (iii) the entire testing process can be completed within 2min.

In order to obtain the optimum reaction conditions, the main experimental factors including reaction time and pH of reaction solution were chosen for investigation [33]. Fig. 4A shows the effect of reaction times on the fluorescence intensity of N-doped CDs. With the addition of 100μL of 10−3 M AA into the N-doped CDs solution, the fluorescence intensity decreased rapidly within 2min and kept stable during the following 20min, implying a promising application in a fast sensing of AA without strict time control. The effect of pH of the IFE-based assay was also investigated by varying PBS buffer solutions (i.e. pH =6.0, 6.8, 7.0 and 7.4) for the detection of 100μL of AA. As shown in Fig. 4B, under the pH of 6.8, N-doped CDs displayed the strongest fluorescence quenching, which was chosen for the further AA sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA with N-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal experimental conditions, convenient and ultrasensitive monitoring of AA could be easily realized. As shown in Fig. 5A, the fluorescence intensity of N-doped CDs decreased as AA concentration increased. A good fitted regression line between the relative fluorescence intensity F0–F (F and F0 refer to the fluorescence intensities of N-doped CDs at 345nm in the presence and absence of AA, respectively) and AA concentrations was in the range of 10−8 to 10−3 M (Fig. 5B), with a linear regression equation as Y =1630.09+188.93logC (C is the concentration of AA (M), R2 =0.9980). The limitation of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 5nM, which was fully capable of sensing trace AA from biological samples. Table 1 shows the developed fluorescence probe with other reported methods. It can be seen that the present method exhibits more superiority including a wider linear range and a lower detection limit, which shows great promise for AA detection in clinic diagnostics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of CDs with different ions and biological molecules
ce:section.ce:para: Good specificity for target analysis was necessary for a newly designed sensing system with potential applications. To evaluate the specificity of the developed IFE-based assay for AA, several potential interfering compounds were investigated under the same conditions. 20μL of various metal ions (200μM) including Zn2+, Pb2+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Al3+ and Ag+ containing N-doped CDs were measured under 345nm excitation. As demonstrated in Fig. S4, the potential interferes containing N-doped CDs caused minor signal fluctuations, indicating the interfering ions showed negligible influence on the fluorescence intensity of N-doped CDs. The selectivity of CDs was further validated by comparing the signal changes of samples containing glutathione (GSH) and several of amino acids (21 kinds). In these experiments, N-doped CDs containing 200μM GSH and amino acids were measured by the assay. The addition of GSH and amino acids triggered negligible signal changes (Fig. S5). The above results showed that the reductive substance such as GSH and Cys did not show interference with the detection of AA. These results showed the detection principle of this approach was different from the traditional redox-based sensing strategies. The proposed method showed a high specificity with ultrafast response and high sensitivity for AA detection, which may be directly applied to detect AA in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the proposed sensing system to serum sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the applications of this method, the proposed sensing system was employed to sense AA in biological fluids. Human serum samples from healthy volunteers (samples 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6) and cancer patients (samples 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12) were screened by the proposed method. Serum samples were obtained from medium-term gastric cancer patient (sample 7), advanced gastric cancer patient (sample 8), medium-term breast cancer patient (sample 9), advanced lung cancer patient (sample 10) and advanced liver cancer patients (sample 11, 12), respectively. The analytical results were shown in Fig. 6. Samples from healthy volunteers (samples 1−6) were 8.77, 8.91, 9.04, 8.76, 8.17 and 8.88μM, respectively, and the average level was 8.75μM. Samples from cancer patients (samples 7−12) were 5.63, 5.49, 6.36, 5.76, 4.38 and 5.08μM, respectively, and the average level was 5.45μM, which was lower than that of the healthy human. These results were almost consistent with the clinical laboratory results and the previous reports [34,35]. Furthermore, the recovery of the method was evaluated by spiking a known amount of AA standard solution (5μM, 10μM, and 20μM) into the serum samples. Serum samples of three healthy volunteers and two cancer patients were selected as the tested samples. As shown in Table 2, the recoveries were ranged from 92.8% to 106.4%, indicating the good accuracy of the proposed sensing method [41,42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the proposed sensing system to rat brain microdialysates analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, AA has been proven to be a kind of neuro molecules, thus monitoring the levels of AA in brain microdialysates are important for the research of physiology and pathology. The fluorescent assay was employed for the detection of AA in rat brain microdialysates of the cortex region. In the process of brain calm/ischemia, 10μL of the rat brain microdialysates were obtained and added into the sensing system, and then the fluorescence intensity was recorded. The brain microdialysate was collected every 10min in the surgeries of the cerebral calm/ischemia process. As shown in Fig. 7, basal levels of cortical AA were 8.07±0.96μM during the calm period (60min); AA levels increased to 10.43±1.63μM during the surgery of preischemia at around 20min and showed a gradual upward trend. Then in the 60min after cerebral ischemia, the AA levels in the cerebral cortex region increased and were determined to be 40.19±2.02μM, which were almost the same as the previous reports [9,12]. Even if there were a lot of potential interferences in biological fluids, the sensor system can distinguish and measure AA from biological fluids. These results clearly demonstrated that the N-doped CD probe could be used as a facile platform for AA screening with excellent selectivity and high sensitivity.

A novel fluorescent assay was developed for the facile detection of AA based on the synergistic effect of IFE and SQE. The highly photoluminescent N-doped CDs were synthesized by a facile and green one-step hydrothermal synthesis method. The as-prepared N-doped CDs has excellent chemical properties such as remarkably stable properties, high water solubility, good biocompatibility and strong fluorescence intensity, which could be served as a novel fluorescence probe for AA sensing. Moreover, the modification of the fluorophore was no longer required in the presented study, allowing a simpler, time-saving and cost-effective way for AA sensing. This fluorescent method yielded a lower detection limit of 5nM and was successfully applied to the detection of AA in serum samples and rat brain microdialysates samples, and satisfactory results were achieved. The proposed sensing strategy has been demonstrated to be a rapid, sensitive, selective and eco-friendly method for AA assay, and provided a potential platform for AA monitoring from many other biological samples.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21677085, 21537001, 81472986, 21305076, 21475074 and 21475075), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2016M590071), The Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2013BQ019 and ZR2016EMB04) and Project of Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (J16LA03).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2017.01.022.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots-based fluorescent probe for turn-on sensing of ascorbic acid
Determination of plasma ascorbic acid by HPLC: method and stability studies
Development and validation of a liquid chromatographic method for the determination of ascorbic acid, dehydroascorbic acid and acetaminophen in pharmaceuticals
Determination of flavonoids and ascorbic acid in grapefruit peel and juice by capillary electrophoresis with electrochemical detection
A selective voltammetric method for uric acid detection at β-cyclodextrin modified electrode incorporating carbon nanotubes
Microwave-assisted synthesis of a core–shell MWCNT/GONR heterostructure for the electrochemical detection of ascorbic acid, dopamine, and uric acid
Facile and Sensitive fluorescence sensing of alkaline phosphatase activity with photoluminescent carbon dots based on inner filter effect
